front: Poem: proverb 'One may lead a horse to water, twenty cannot make him drink'
Poem: ends with the moral 'for there is no friend like a sister'
Poem: Laura uses 'a golden curl' and 'a tear more rare than pearl' to buy forbidden fruit from goblins
Poem: title creatures described as 'cat-like and rat-like, ratel- and wombat-like'
Poem: one 'had a cat's face,' another 'tramp'd at a rat's pace'
Poem: woman invites 'Did you miss me? / Come and kiss me' and 'Eat me, drink me, love me' — sexually charged language
Poem: cautionary figure Jeanie 'fell sick and died' — in 'her grave, who should have been a bride'
Poem: fruit catalogue includes 'Apples and quinces, / Lemons and oranges, / Plump unpeck'd cherries, / Melons and raspberries'
Poem: The Madwoman in the Attic compares Laura's desire for fruit to Keats's 'La Belle Dame Sans Merci'
Poem: Elsie Norris reads this poem to Jeanette during a hospital stay in Oranges Are Not the Only Fruit
Poem: Christmas carol; 'frosty wind make moan' / 'Earth stood hard as iron' / 'snow on snow, snow on snow'
Poem: 'If I were a Shepherd, I would bring a lamb'
Poem: 'Our God, heaven cannot hold Him / Nor earth sustain'
Poem: originally titled 'A Christmas Carol'
Poem: 'Better by far you should forget and smile' — speaker imagines herself 'Gone far away into the silent land'
Poem: fears 'darkness and corruption' leaving 'A vestige of the thoughts that once I had'
Poem: opens with 'There's blood between us, love'
Poem: guilty speaker recalls 'You sinned with me a pleasant sin'
Poem: speaker dreams of 'fire-footed' 'spirit with transfigured face' screaming 'Give me light'
Poem: spirit 'drank a sea' seeking knowledge, 'to learn that all is small / Save love, for love is all in all'
Poem: dialogue poem consisting entirely of one-line questions and answers
Poem: 'Will there be beds for me and all who seek?'
Poem: speaker's heart compared to 'a singing bird,' 'an apple tree,' and 'a rainbow shell,' 'gladder than all these' because 'my love has come to me'
Poem: couple 'went together in glowing August weather'
Poem: narrator sees 'a scaled and hooded worm' lurking in flowers
Poem: love triangle — Maude Clare compared to 'queen,' bride to village maid
Poem: 'Take my share of a fickle heart' / 'I'll love him till he loves me best'
Author: 'The One Certainty' — 'there is nothing new under the sun' / 'All things are vanity'
Author: 'Symbols' — crushing three 'failed' eggs
Author: God 'waiteth for thy fruits in vain'
Author: 'De Profundis' (not Oscar Wilde's) — 'I strain my heart, I stretch my hands, / and catch at hope'
Author: wrote 'The P.R.B.,' a sonnet comparing the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood's decline to 'luscious fruit [that] must fall when over-ripe'
Poem: Laura plants a "kernel-stone" watered with tears — never sprouts
Poem: Dante Gabriel Rossetti provided illustrations for the first edition
